question: Why do you want to study dentistry rather than medicine or another health profession?
lookingFor: A specific, credible motivation grounded in real exposure that distinguishes dentistry from adjacent health careers.

bullets: Articulate what specifically draws you to dentistry over medicine or other allied fields
Back the claim with direct experience — work shadowing, personal dental care, volunteering
Show awareness of what a dentist's day-to-day actually looks like
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I spent two weeks shadowing a general dental practice last summer, and what struck me was the immediacy of it — a patient comes in anxious, and by the time they leave you've actually fixed the problem. That loop of diagnosis, precise manual work, and visible outcome appeals to me far more than the longer diagnostic cycles in medicine. I also find the anatomy of the mouth — the intersection of aesthetics, function, and systemic health — genuinely fascinating. The combination of clinical science with the fine motor skill side is unlike anything else in healthcare, and I want to spend my career at that intersection.

commonMistakes: Saying 'I want to help people' with no specificity about why dentistry
Implying medicine was the real goal but dentistry was more achievable
No direct exposure to dentistry to draw on
followUps: What surprised you most when you actually spent time in a dental setting?

question: Tell us about your work experience or shadowing in dentistry. What did you take away from it?
lookingFor: Genuine reflection on what the experience taught them about dentistry — not just a description of what they observed.

bullets: Describe a specific moment or procedure that stuck with them
Reflect critically — what confirmed, challenged, or surprised them
Connect what they saw to a quality or interest they bring to the course
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I shadowed in a mixed NHS and private practice for three weeks. The moment that stayed with me most was watching the dentist handle a patient with severe dental anxiety — she'd spent years avoiding treatment. The dentist slowed everything right down, narrated each step before doing it, and the patient left having had a full examination for the first time in seven years. It made me realise that the communication skill matters as much as the clinical skill. I also watched some basic restorations up close and was surprised how tactile and precise the work is — you're working in millimetres in a wet, confined space. Both of those realities made me more certain, not less.

commonMistakes: Only describing what they saw without any personal reflection
Treating work experience as a box to tick rather than a learning experience
Being unable to name a specific procedure or patient interaction
followUps: Was there anything you observed that you found difficult or uncomfortable?

question: Why have you applied to this dental school in particular?
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine research into this specific school — named features, clinical exposure patterns, or teaching philosophy — not just prestige.

bullets: Name a specific feature of the curriculum, clinic setup, or teaching approach
Connect it to how you learn or what you want from training
Show you know something about how this school differs from others
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I was drawn to the early patient contact your curriculum offers — students are treating patients in supervised clinics from year two, which I think is the right way to build clinical confidence. I read about your problem-based learning approach and felt it matched how I'd retained material best in my A-level studies — working through a real case rather than just absorbing theory. I also looked at your community outreach programme in underserved areas, which matters to me because accessible dental care is something I want to be part of building a career around.

commonMistakes: Generic praise that could fit any dental school
Mentioning only league table position or reputation
Confusing this school's programme structure with another school's
followUps: What do you think you'd contribute to the student community here?

question: Dentistry requires exceptional manual dexterity. How have you developed or demonstrated yours?
lookingFor: Concrete evidence of fine motor skill — hobbies, crafts, instruments, art — plus self-awareness about what precision demands feel like.

bullets: Name specific activities that genuinely require fine motor control
Describe a moment that tested that precision — not just 'I play guitar'
Connect the skill to what dentistry requires
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've played violin since I was eight, and I think it's shaped my hands in ways I only fully appreciated when I started looking at dentistry seriously. Bowing requires sustained fine control under pressure — if your hand tightens, you hear it immediately in the sound. I've also spent the last two years doing model-making, working with 1:72 scale figures that need incredibly fine brushwork. When I watched restorations being placed during shadowing, I recognised the same kind of focused, small-movement work. I won't pretend the scale is the same, but I felt less intimidated than I expected.

commonMistakes: Claiming good dexterity with no specific activity to support it
Listing hobbies without connecting them to the demands of dental work
Being vague about what precision actually requires
followUps: How do you think you'll handle working in the confined space of the mouth?

question: Can you tell us about any links between oral health and general systemic health?
lookingFor: Awareness that dentistry sits within the wider body — a clear example with some understanding of the mechanism, not just a name-drop.

bullets: Give at least one specific well-evidenced link — cardiovascular disease, diabetes, or similar
Sketch the biological mechanism without over-claiming
Show this excites rather than overwhelms them — it widens the scope of what a dentist does
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The link between periodontal disease and cardiovascular disease is one I read about in some depth. Chronic gum disease drives persistent low-grade inflammation, and there's evidence that the bacteria involved can enter the bloodstream and contribute to the inflammatory burden on vessel walls. There's also a bidirectional relationship with diabetes — poorly controlled blood sugar worsens gum disease, and periodontal inflammation makes blood sugar harder to control. What I find interesting is that dentists see patients more frequently than GPs for many people, so a dentist who picks up signs of systemic disease early can genuinely change a patient's outcome beyond just their oral health.

commonMistakes: Naming a link without any explanation of how or why
Treating the mouth as entirely separate from the rest of the body
Over-claiming certainty on associations that are still being researched
followUps: How might that link change what you look for when examining a patient?

question: Can you walk us through how dental caries develops?
lookingFor: A clear mechanistic account — bacteria, substrate, acid, demineralisation — delivered in the student's own words rather than a textbook recitation.

bullets: Identify the bacteria involved and what they act on
Explain the acid-demineralisation process clearly
Note the role of saliva and fluoride as protective factors
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Caries develops when bacteria in dental plaque — mainly Streptococcus mutans — metabolise dietary sugars and produce acid as a byproduct. That acid lowers the pH around the tooth, and below a pH of about 5.5 the enamel starts to demineralise — calcium and phosphate ions leach out of the crystal lattice. If that happens repeatedly and remineralisation can't keep up, the structural integrity of the enamel breaks down and a cavity forms. Saliva is the key defence — it buffers the acid and provides the minerals needed to remineralise. Fluoride helps by forming fluorapatite, which is more acid-resistant than the natural hydroxyapatite. That's why fluoride toothpaste and diet are such central messages in prevention.

commonMistakes: Describing caries as 'sugar rotting teeth' without the bacterial and biochemical mechanism
Forgetting the remineralisation side of the equation
Being unable to name any bacteria involved
followUps: What preventive advice would you give a patient who keeps getting new cavities?

question: What do you understand about the differences between NHS and private dental care in the UK?
lookingFor: Basic structural literacy about how dental services are funded and delivered — and an honest view on the implications for patients.

bullets: Explain the band system for NHS charges and what it covers
Contrast with private care: patient fees, flexibility, materials choices
Acknowledge the access and equity issues this creates
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: NHS dentistry in England is organised around three charge bands: Band 1 covers examination, X-rays, and scale and polish; Band 2 covers most restorations, extractions, and root canal work; Band 3 covers dentures, crowns, and bridges. The NHS contract pays practices on a units-of-dental-activity basis, which critics argue creates pressure to see volume over quality and limits the time dentists can spend per patient. Private dentistry is fee-per-item and gives more freedom in materials and treatment planning — for example, tooth-coloured composites for posterior fillings are routine privately but may not be provided on the NHS. The real issue is access: NHS dentist capacity has been shrinking, and in many areas patients simply can't find an NHS practice taking new patients. That creates a genuine inequality I think about when I consider where I want to practise.

commonMistakes: Having no awareness of how NHS dentistry is funded
Treating the difference as simply 'free versus not free'
Ignoring the access crisis or speaking about it in the abstract
followUps: If you were the next health secretary for a day, what one change would you make to NHS dentistry?

question: A patient refuses a treatment you believe is clearly in their best interest. How do you approach that?
lookingFor: Understanding of patient autonomy and valid consent — not paternalism, but also not passive acceptance without communication.

bullets: Start from the principle that a competent adult's decision must be respected
Describe what you'd do to ensure the decision is truly informed
Explain how you'd document and follow up
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The starting point has to be that a competent patient has the right to refuse any treatment — that's not a failure, that's the system working as it should. What I'd want to do first is make sure the refusal is truly informed: do they understand what the tooth is likely to do if left untreated, and what the options are? I'd go through the risks and the alternatives calmly, ask if there were specific concerns I could address, and offer to give them time to decide. If they still refused, I'd respect that, document it clearly, and make sure they knew they could return. My role is to give the best advice I can — the decision is theirs.

commonMistakes: Suggesting you'd push or persuade a patient to override their decision
Agreeing immediately without checking the decision is informed
Forgetting to document the conversation
followUps: Does your answer change if the patient is a 15-year-old?

question: Dental anxiety is very common. How do you think you'd handle an anxious patient?
lookingFor: Empathy grounded in practical technique — not just 'I'd be kind', but specific communication strategies they've thought about.

bullets: Acknowledge the anxiety as legitimate, not as an obstacle to manage
Describe specific approaches: tell-show-do, stop signals, pacing
Show they'd adapt their style rather than apply a formula
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The first thing I'd want to avoid is making the patient feel like their anxiety is a problem I need to get past. A lot of dental fear comes from past experiences of feeling out of control, so giving some control back really matters. Tell-show-do is a technique I read about and saw used during shadowing — you explain each step, show the instrument, then carry out the procedure — and it reduces the element of surprise significantly. I'd also offer a clear stop signal, even just a raised hand, so they know they can pause things at any moment. I think the pace changes too — some patients just need the whole appointment to move more slowly, and building time for that matters.

commonMistakes: Treating anxiety as irrational or something to push through
Offering no specific technique — just 'I'd be nice and calm'
Not mentioning the patient's ability to stop or pause
followUps: Have you ever experienced a situation where you had to manage someone's anxiety — not dental, but in any context?

question: What areas of dentistry are you aware of, beyond general practice?
lookingFor: A realistic picture of the specialty landscape — shows the applicant has looked beyond the high street practice.

bullets: Name at least three or four recognised specialties with a brief description of each
Show some genuine interest in one area without over-committing
Demonstrate awareness that most dentists begin in general practice
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I know the main specialties include orthodontics — correcting teeth and jaw alignment, which is one of the more visible areas to the public — and oral and maxillofacial surgery, which sits at the interface of dentistry and medicine and involves treating conditions from oral cancer to facial trauma. Periodontics focuses on the supporting structures of the teeth, and endodontics covers root canal treatment in depth. There's also paediatric dentistry, which interests me because building a positive dental experience for children has lifelong preventive value. I know most dentists start in general practice and may move into specialist training or further training later, and I think that generalist foundation is valuable before narrowing down.

commonMistakes: Only knowing about general practice and orthodontics
Claiming a specialty interest with no understanding of what it involves
Treating specialties as just 'harder versions' of general dentistry
followUps: Is there an area you're curious about exploring further, even if you're unsure right now?

question: A patient calls saying they have severe toothache and want to come in today. What would you want to find out before the appointment?
lookingFor: Early clinical reasoning instincts — they don't need to diagnose but should know what information matters and why.

bullets: Ask about the nature and location of pain — constant versus intermittent, sharp versus dull
Ask about swelling, temperature sensitivity, and any systemic symptoms
Establish urgency — particularly whether there is facial swelling that might signal spreading infection
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd want to understand the pain first: is it constant or does it come and go, what sets it off, how severe is it? That helps narrow things down — constant severe pain that wakes you up can suggest pulpitis or a periapical abscess, while sharp pain that settles quickly after a cold stimulus might be sensitivity. I'd also ask specifically about any swelling, either in the mouth or the face, because facial swelling can indicate a spreading infection that might need more urgent management, possibly with antibiotics or referral rather than just a restoration. And I'd ask about any fever or difficulty swallowing, which would raise urgency further. You don't need a diagnosis on the phone, but you need to know how quickly this person actually needs to be seen.

commonMistakes: Treating all toothache as equal urgency
Only asking about the pain without asking about swelling or systemic signs
Trying to diagnose over the phone rather than triaging
followUps: What would make you refer this patient to A&E rather than treating them yourself?

question: What is fluoride and why is it important in dentistry?
lookingFor: A clear basic explanation of fluoride's mechanism and its role in prevention — not a policy debate, but the science.

bullets: Explain how fluoride is incorporated into tooth structure
Describe how it helps prevent and reverse early caries
Mention sources — toothpaste, water fluoridation, varnish
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Fluoride is a mineral that gets incorporated into tooth enamel during development and also at the surface in adult teeth. It works mainly by replacing hydroxyl groups in the hydroxyapatite crystal lattice to form fluorapatite, which is more resistant to the acid produced by oral bacteria. At low concentrations it also promotes remineralisation of early enamel lesions — so it can actually reverse caries in its early stages, not just prevent new ones. The main delivery routes are toothpaste, which is the most impactful at a population level, fluoride varnish applied clinically — especially useful in children at high caries risk — and water fluoridation in areas where it's implemented. It's one of the most evidence-based tools in preventive dentistry.

commonMistakes: Describing fluoride only as something added to water without explaining why
Confusing fluoride's mechanism with it being antimicrobial
Getting sidetracked into the political debate about water fluoridation
followUps: What would you say to a parent who's worried about giving their child fluoride toothpaste?

question: Why is cross-infection control so important in a dental setting, and what does it involve?
lookingFor: Genuine understanding of infection risk in dentistry — blood, aerosols, instruments — and the layered approach to control.

bullets: Explain why dentistry carries specific infection risks — blood, aerosol-generating procedures
Describe the key control measures: PPE, sterilisation, single-use items, decontamination protocols
Show awareness that this protects both patients and the dental team
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Dental procedures routinely involve blood and generate aerosols that carry saliva, blood, and potentially infectious material into the air and onto surfaces. That creates real transmission routes for blood-borne viruses like HIV and hepatitis B and C, as well as respiratory pathogens. The response is layered: personal protective equipment — gloves, masks, eye protection, visors — protects the team during procedures. Instruments are either single-use and disposed of, or they go through a full decontamination cycle: cleaning, sterilisation in an autoclave, and tracked storage. Surfaces are covered or wiped between patients. There are detailed guidelines from bodies like the CQC and SDCEP in Scotland that set the standard. What I find important about this is that it protects everyone — the patient from the previous patient, and the team from the patients they treat every day.

commonMistakes: Only mentioning gloves and hand washing without the full picture
Forgetting that the dental team is also at risk, not just patients
Not knowing what an autoclave does
followUps: What would you do if you saw a colleague skip a step in the decontamination process?

question: A 19-year-old patient with perfectly healthy teeth asks you to shave them down for veneers purely for cosmetic reasons. How do you handle that?
lookingFor: Ethical reasoning that balances patient autonomy against the principle of do no harm — irreversible treatment on healthy tissue.

bullets: Flag the irreversible nature of tooth preparation — this is not a neutral act on healthy teeth
Respect autonomy but ensure fully informed consent including long-term risks
Explore whether less invasive alternatives could meet their goals
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: This is one of the genuinely difficult tensions in aesthetic dentistry. The patient is an adult with decision-making capacity, so I take their goals seriously — but preparing healthy teeth for veneers means removing enamel that cannot grow back, and those teeth will need crowns or replacement veneers for life. My first responsibility is to make sure the consent is truly informed: they need to understand the irreversibility, the risks — pulp exposure, sensitivity, future crown failure — and the lifelong commitment. I'd also want to explore whether composite bonding or whitening could give them what they want without destructive preparation. If after a full conversation they still want to proceed and understand the risks, the ethical framework around autonomy does support that decision. But I wouldn't be comfortable rushing to it, and I'd want time to check this isn't a decision driven by image distress that might benefit from another kind of support first.

commonMistakes: Refusing outright without acknowledging patient autonomy
Agreeing without any discussion of the risks and irreversibility
Not considering less invasive alternatives
followUps: Would your answer be different if the patient was 16?

question: Have you read about any recent developments or innovations in dentistry that interest you?
lookingFor: Genuine curiosity and engagement with the field beyond the textbook — a specific development they can speak about with some depth.

bullets: Name a specific development — digital dentistry, biomaterials, AI diagnostics, or similar
Explain what it does and why it matters in terms they understand
Connect it to why they find it exciting
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been reading about the use of AI in radiographic analysis — software that can detect early caries or periapical lesions in bitewing X-rays with accuracy that compares well with experienced clinicians. What interests me is the potential to reduce inter-observer variation — the fact that the same X-ray can be read differently by different dentists on different days is a real limitation, and a calibrated AI tool could act as a second reader consistently. I'm also aware there are important questions around liability and how much the dentist should still be the primary decision-maker. It doesn't feel like a threat to the profession so much as a tool that could raise the floor of diagnostic quality, especially in settings without specialist support.

commonMistakes: Giving a vague answer about '3D printing' or 'technology' with no specific detail
Reading about something without being able to explain how it works
Uncritically praising a technology without any nuance about limitations
followUps: Do you think AI tools should be able to make clinical decisions autonomously in dentistry?

question: Who makes up the dental team, and why does effective teamwork matter in a dental setting?
lookingFor: Awareness of the wider dental team — nurses, hygienists, therapists, receptionists — and a genuine understanding of why team function affects patient care.

bullets: Name the key roles beyond the dentist
Give a concrete example of how team coordination affects patient outcomes
Show awareness that the dentist is not always the most important person in the room
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The dental team includes the dentist, dental nurses, hygienists, therapists, receptionists, and in larger practices a practice manager. During shadowing I noticed how much of the patient experience is shaped before they even sit in the chair — the receptionist sets the tone, and an anxious patient who's greeted well arrives calmer. The dental nurse is essential to everything running smoothly in the surgery: anticipating instruments, maintaining suction, monitoring the patient. Hygienists and therapists carry a large part of the prevention and periodontal care load. A dentist who treats the nurse as invisible or doesn't communicate well with the front desk creates friction that the patient feels. I think one of the things dental school needs to teach is how to be a good team member, not just a good clinician.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the dental nurse or treating them as an assistant rather than a skilled team member
Not mentioning hygienists or therapists
Focusing only on clinical roles and ignoring the administrative and reception team
followUps: Tell us about a time you worked well as part of a team in any context.

question: Do you think dentistry should be more focused on prevention than treatment? What's your view?
lookingFor: A considered position that shows the applicant has thought about the tension between reactive treatment and population health — not a simple yes.

bullets: Acknowledge that both are essential and that prevention reduces the demand for treatment
Raise the challenge of health inequalities — prevention requires engagement the most vulnerable may not have
Show they understand the dentist's role in prevention as a clinical encounter, not just a public health message
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Prevention is clearly the right direction — treating a restored tooth costs far more in time and money than a fluoride varnish and dietary advice, and the human cost of untreated dental disease is significant. But I think 'more prevention' is easier to say than to deliver. The populations with the highest caries burden are often the hardest to reach with behaviour-change messages: lower-income households, children in areas without fluoridated water, people who've avoided dentistry for years. So prevention has to work at multiple levels — the individual clinical encounter, yes, but also policy levers like water fluoridation, school toothbrushing programmes, and reformulation of foods. The dentist in the chair can deliver prevention, and that's important, but it can't be the only mechanism.

commonMistakes: Giving a one-word answer without engaging with the tension
Treating prevention as obviously superior with no acknowledgment of the complexity
Being unaware of any population-level prevention tools
followUps: What's one policy change you think would have the biggest impact on dental public health?

question: Imagine you are a qualified dentist and you accidentally damage a healthy adjacent tooth during a restoration. What do you do?
lookingFor: Honesty, patient-centred thinking, and duty of candour — an applicant who understands that transparency is not optional in healthcare.

bullets: Disclose to the patient immediately — the duty of candour is clear
Document what happened accurately and take responsibility
Arrange appropriate remedial treatment and consider reporting through practice governance
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The first thing I'd do is be honest with the patient before they leave the chair — explain clearly what happened, apologise, and tell them what the next steps are to address it. That's not just the ethical thing to do, it's a professional and legal obligation under the duty of candour. Hiding it is not an option — the patient has a right to know, and concealment would compound the harm significantly. I'd document everything accurately in the notes, arrange any necessary remedial treatment, and discuss it with a senior colleague or the practice owner. Depending on severity I might also need to report through clinical governance. It would be a difficult conversation, but the right one. Patients are far more forgiving of honest mistakes than of cover-ups.

commonMistakes: Suggesting they would try to fix it quietly without telling the patient
Focusing only on the clinical fix without addressing disclosure
Being vague about the duty of candour or not knowing it exists
followUps: What if a colleague told you to say nothing because the patient probably wouldn't notice?

question: Why is the dentist's role in oral cancer detection important, and what are the main risk factors?
lookingFor: Awareness of oral cancer as a significant health issue that dentists are often first to detect — with at least the key risk factors named.

bullets: Explain that dentists see patients regularly and are positioned to spot early lesions
Name the main risk factors: tobacco, alcohol, HPV
Stress the importance of early detection for outcomes
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Oral cancer has a relatively poor prognosis partly because it's often caught late — and that's where the dentist's role is critical. We see patients regularly, we're examining the inside of the mouth as part of routine care, and we can identify suspicious lesions — ulcers that haven't healed in three weeks, white or red patches, unexplained lumps — that might easily be missed otherwise. The major risk factors are tobacco in all forms, heavy alcohol use, and increasingly high-risk strains of HPV, particularly in pharyngeal cancers. The combination of alcohol and tobacco is especially synergistic. Five-year survival rates are considerably higher for early-stage disease, so a routine soft tissue examination is one of the highest-value things a dentist can do. I think a lot of patients don't know their dental check-up includes a cancer screen.

commonMistakes: Not knowing HPV as a risk factor, only tobacco and alcohol
Being unaware that dentists have a specific role in oral cancer detection
Treating oral cancer as rare without acknowledging it's actually relatively common
followUps: What would you do if you spotted a suspicious lesion during a routine examination?

question: Dentistry is a demanding degree and a demanding career. What challenges do you anticipate, and how do you think you'll manage them?
lookingFor: Realistic awareness of the demands — academic rigour, emotional labour, physical posture, communication — with a credible personal account of their coping resources.

bullets: Name specific challenges that are realistic to dentistry — not generic 'hard work'
Show they've thought about how they personally cope with stress or difficulty
Avoid either bravado ('I'll be fine') or catastrophising
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think the clinical load will be demanding in ways I've only partly seen from shadowing — managing a full patient list while maintaining precision and keeping a nervous patient calm is a lot to hold at once. I also know the physical demands are significant: dental posture risks musculoskeletal problems if you're not careful, and that's something I want to be disciplined about from the start. Emotionally, I think delivering bad news — an untreatable tooth, a suspicious lesion, or an anxious child who's really struggling — will be harder than the clinical work. I manage stress reasonably well by staying physically active and being deliberate about switching off; I've also found in other high-pressure settings like music performance that preparation is my best anxiety management. I'm not under the illusion that this is easy, but I think I've chosen it with open eyes.

commonMistakes: Listing only academic difficulty without acknowledging emotional or physical demands
Claiming they handle stress with no personal example or evidence
Being so confident about managing challenges that it sounds unaware
followUps: Tell us about a time you had to cope with something genuinely difficult. What helped?

question: Can you explain what periodontal disease is and why it matters?
lookingFor: A basic mechanistic account of gum disease — plaque, inflammation, bone loss — and awareness of its significance including links to systemic health.

bullets: Explain the progression from gingivitis to periodontitis
Describe bone and attachment loss as the key irreversible feature
Note the systemic links and the scale of the problem globally
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Periodontal disease starts as gingivitis — inflammation of the gums caused by the bacterial biofilm in plaque, which provokes an immune response. At this stage the damage is reversible with good plaque control. When the inflammation isn't resolved, it can progress to periodontitis, where the inflammatory process starts destroying the periodontal ligament and the alveolar bone supporting the teeth. That bone loss is generally irreversible, and over time it can lead to tooth mobility and tooth loss. The bacteria involved can also enter the systemic circulation, which is why periodontitis is linked to conditions like cardiovascular disease and diabetes. What surprised me when I read about it is the scale — severe periodontitis affects around 10% of adults globally, making it one of the most prevalent chronic diseases there is. Early identification and treatment by scaling, cleaning, and improving patient plaque control can halt progression, but you can't regrow lost bone easily.

commonMistakes: Treating gingivitis and periodontitis as interchangeable
Forgetting that periodontitis involves bone loss, not just inflamed gums
Being unaware of any systemic connections
followUps: What home care advice would you give a patient with early gum disease?

question: A patient tells you in the chair that they've been experiencing domestic abuse at home. How do you handle that?
lookingFor: Understanding of confidentiality, safeguarding duties, and the limits of professional discretion — especially when a third party may be at risk.

bullets: Acknowledge and validate the disclosure before anything else
Explain that confidentiality is the default but has limits under safeguarding law
Show they'd seek advice from a supervisor or safeguarding lead before acting
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The first thing I'd do is acknowledge what they've told me and make sure they feel heard — this person has chosen to tell me something significant, and that matters. I wouldn't rush to clinical mode. In terms of confidentiality, the default is always to protect the patient's information — but there are limits, particularly where there's a real risk of serious harm to the patient or someone else, such as children in the household. I'm not trained to make those safeguarding decisions alone as a newly qualified dentist, so my next step would be to speak with the practice's designated safeguarding lead, which every practice should have, and get their guidance on what's required. I'd also make sure the patient knows about support services and that the conversation has been recorded appropriately. Acting unilaterally or doing nothing are both wrong; this is exactly the kind of situation where you use your team.

commonMistakes: Treating it as a confidentiality matter only, ignoring the safeguarding dimension
Deciding alone whether or how to report without involving a safeguarding lead
Failing to acknowledge and respond humanly to the disclosure before thinking procedurally
followUps: What if the patient asks you to promise not to tell anyone before they disclose?

question: What do you know about how local anaesthesia works in dentistry?
lookingFor: A basic but accurate account of how local anaesthetics block nerve conduction — demonstrates scientific curiosity beyond GCSE biology.

bullets: Explain the mechanism: sodium channel blockade preventing action potential propagation
Name a commonly used agent — lidocaine — and mention vasoconstrictor use
Show awareness of why it occasionally doesn't work as expected
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Local anaesthetics work by blocking voltage-gated sodium channels in nerve membranes. Normally, a pain signal is carried as an action potential along a sensory nerve by rapid sodium influx. The anaesthetic molecule — lidocaine is the most common in dentistry — diffuses into the nerve and occupies those sodium channels, preventing them from opening. Without the sodium influx, the action potential can't propagate and the signal doesn't reach the brain. Dental solutions usually contain a vasoconstrictor like adrenaline, which constricts the local blood supply and keeps the anaesthetic in place longer rather than being washed away. One interesting thing is that local anaesthetics work better in normal tissue — in an acutely infected area, the lower pH affects how well the drug crosses the nerve membrane, which is why anaesthetising an abscess can be genuinely harder.

commonMistakes: Describing local anaesthesia as simply 'numbing' without any mechanism
Not knowing that a vasoconstrictor is usually included
Being unable to explain why anaesthesia can fail in infected tissue
followUps: What would you do if a patient's local anaesthetic wasn't working after two injections?

question: A parent insists you extract a child's decayed baby tooth rather than restore it, against the child's expressed wishes. How do you approach this?
lookingFor: Ethical reasoning that considers the child's evolving capacity, parental rights, clinical best interest, and the importance of building trust.

bullets: Acknowledge both the parent's rights and the child's emerging autonomy
Consider clinical factors — is extraction or restoration actually the right treatment?
Prioritise explaining and building trust with the child, not just complying with the parent
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: This is a genuinely complex situation. The parent has legal responsibility for the child's care, but a child's expressed wishes and their developing capacity still matter — we take Gillick competence seriously in healthcare, and even below that threshold a child's distress at a procedure they're resisting has real consequences for their future relationship with dental care. My first step would be to think clinically: is extraction actually the right treatment for this tooth, or is restoration more appropriate? If restoration is clinically justifiable, I'd advocate for it. I'd also spend time with the child directly — explaining what each option feels like, building some trust, and asking what's worrying them. A forced extraction on a frightened child who didn't want it can cause dental anxiety that lasts decades. Ultimately if the parent insists and extraction is clinically reasonable, I'd proceed — but carefully, with consent fully documented and ideally with the child as on board as possible.

commonMistakes: Simply deferring to the parent without considering the child's perspective at all
Refusing to treat without any nuance about parental rights
Ignoring the long-term consequence of traumatising a child in the dental chair
followUps: At what age do you think a child should start making their own dental decisions?

question: Dentistry is a field that keeps evolving. How do you plan to stay up to date once you qualify?
lookingFor: Awareness that qualification is not the endpoint — the applicant understands continuing professional development as a professional obligation and a genuine interest.

bullets: Name CPD as a GDC requirement — shows regulatory awareness
Describe specific ways they'd stay current: journals, courses, peer discussion
Connect it to their genuine interest in the field rather than treating it as a compliance chore
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The GDC requires registered dentists to complete continuing professional development — at least 100 hours over a five-year cycle, including mandatory topics like medical emergencies and infection control. But I think the framing of CPD as a compliance requirement undersells it. Dentistry is moving quickly — digital workflows, new biomaterials, better evidence on caries management — and the dentist I'd want to be is one who's genuinely curious about what's changed. I plan to read the BDJ and similar journals, go to study days and clinical conferences, and make sure I'm having conversations with colleagues whose skills are different from mine. I also think peer review and case discussion with colleagues is some of the most useful CPD you can do, because it surfaces blind spots that formal learning doesn't. I hope to be the kind of dentist who's still excited by a new technique at 40.

commonMistakes: Not knowing that CPD is a GDC regulatory requirement
Describing learning as something you do to pass exams, not as a career-long commitment
Being vague about any specific sources or methods for staying current
followUps: Is there a specific area of dentistry where you feel the evidence base is changing fastest right now?
